---
title: "Contribution of the Late Positive Potential to Emotional Memory: A Joint Neuro-Cognitive Approach to Memory Prediction"
shorttitle: "LPP Contribution to Emotional Memory"
author:
  - name: Robin Hellerstedt
    affiliations:
      - name: University
  - name: Jordan Gunn
    affiliations:
      - name: University of Cambridge
  - name: Deborah Talmi
    affiliations:
      - name: University of Cambridge
bibliography: references.bib
---

## Computational modeling of the Early-LPP contribution to emotional recall

### From empirical association to computational mechanism

The preceding behavioral and EEG analyses established two regularities that operate at different levels. Negative items were recalled more often than neutral items, and Early LPP distinguished subsequently Remembered from Forgotten items more strongly among negative than neutral items. The first regularity concerns an average difference between categories; the second concerns which items were selected for recall within a category. A statistical association can establish these regularities, but it cannot show how a measured neural response might alter the operations of a memory system. We therefore asked whether category membership and item-level Early LPP make redundant or complementary contributions when both are expressed within a process model of recall.

The model comparison was designed to answer three questions in sequence. First, does preferential learning of negative items improve prediction of recalled-item identity? Second, does Early LPP add predictive information beyond a model with no EEG input? Third, is that information better represented by one LPP relationship shared across negative and neutral items, or by an LPP relationship concentrated among negative items? These questions motivate a controlled (2 \times 3) comparison rather than a search across unrelated model architectures. To understand the comparison, we first explain the common memory mechanism through which category and Early LPP can affect recall.

### A reduced eCMR framework

We used a reduced version of emotional Context Maintenance and Retrieval (eCMR) [@talmi2019retrieved]. Like other retrieved-context models [@howard2002distributed; @polyn2009context], eCMR assumes that studying an item changes a gradually evolving internal context and binds the item to that context. During free recall, the current context cues studied items; a retrieved item then reinstates aspects of its study context, which can cue further items. Encoding strength is therefore a natural computational bridge between an item-level neural measurement and later recall: changing the strength of an item's context-to-item association changes the support that the item receives during retrieval. That bridge is clearest once category representation is distinguished from preferential category-based learning.

Every model in the primary comparison represented temporal information and negative-versus-neutral source information as components of one contextual state. Negative items carried one source feature and neutral items another, allowing retrieval to reinstate category-matched source information. This source representation was present in all six models, including models without preferential learning of negative items. Consequently, “categorical enhancement absent” means that negative and neutral items had the same source learning multiplier; it does not mean that the model lacked category labels or source features. The comparison instead varied how strongly items were bound to the source component of context.

At study, each item first updated the temporal and source components of context and was then associated with that updated context. This post-update sequence ensures that an item's associations include the contextual state produced by the item itself. At recall, temporal and source context-to-item cues were summed, scaled by a common choice-sensitivity parameter, and normalized across the items still available for recall. Stronger source context-to-item learning therefore increased an item's retrieval support when a compatible source state was active. The present comparison altered this source learning strength while leaving the temporal context-to-item learning rule unchanged.

The implementation retained the eCMR machinery required for that comparison but should not be read as the complete published model. It did not represent pre-experimental semantic similarity among the pictures, and it omitted the source-switch disruption of temporal context used to model category transitions in full eCMR. Source-context drift during encoding was estimated, whereas source-context reinstatement during recall was fixed to fully reflect the just-retrieved item's source feature. Repeated recalls were disallowed. Disclosing these choices before the comparison is important: any result below concerns this reduced source-context implementation, not every mechanism proposed in eCMR. Within the shared implementation, the six models differed only in the categorical and Early-LPP terms applied to source context-to-item learning.

### Encoding-strength hypotheses

The common source learning baseline for item (i) was (\omega\phi_i). The position-dependent term (\phi_i = 1 + \phi_s\exp[-\phi_d(i-1)]) implements the ordinary primacy gradient, and (\omega) scales source learning relative to temporal learning. The source scale (\omega) was estimated in every model rather than fixed, so each model could accommodate the overall contribution of source-context associations before either focal mechanism was introduced. The first focal mechanism then asked whether an item's category should multiply that ordinary learning strength.

Categorical preferential learning was represented by a multiplier (\phi_{\mathrm{emot}}). For a negative item, the source learning baseline was multiplied by (\phi_{\mathrm{emot}}); for a neutral item, it was unchanged. Models without this mechanism fixed (\phi_{\mathrm{emot}}=1), its neutral value, whereas models with the mechanism estimated the multiplier. The parameter bounds allowed either attenuation or enhancement, although the fitted values reported below were greater than 1. A single category multiplier can change the average recall advantage for negative items, but it cannot distinguish two negative items that elicited different Early-LPP amplitudes. The second focal mechanism supplied that item-level variation.

For each item, the model used within-list mean-centered Early LPP,

\[
z_{ij}=\mathrm{EarlyLPP}_{ij}-\overline{\mathrm{EarlyLPP}}_{j},
\]

where (j) indexes a 20-item study-test list. Thus, (z_{ij}) indicates whether an item elicited a larger or smaller pre-stimulus-standardized Early LPP than its listmates. Centering aligns the predictor with the conditional question addressed by the likelihood: which items are selected from a particular list? It also removes between-list mean-LPP variation by construction, so these models cannot test whether lists with larger average LPPs yield more recalls. Because the preceding empirical model did not separate within-list and between-list LPP variation, the present coefficients are a computational mapping of the within-list pattern rather than numerical counterparts of the empirical regression coefficients. We next specify how this signed predictor changed learning without allowing learning strength to become negative.

The three influences combined in one positive source learning-strength rule:

\[
S_i^S
=
\omega\phi_i
\left[1+(\phi_{\mathrm{emot}}-1)e_i\right]
\exp\!\left(\kappa z_i+\kappa_{\mathrm{emot}}e_i z_i\right),
\]

where (e_i=1) for a negative item and (e_i=0) for a neutral item. The categorical bracket equals (\phi_{\mathrm{emot}}) for negative items and 1 for neutral items. The exponential term makes (\kappa) the common Early-LPP slope on log learning strength and (\kappa_{\mathrm{emot}}) an additional negative-item slope. Exponentiation guarantees positive learning strength, but the log link is more than a numerical safeguard: it assumes that a unit change in Early LPP produces a proportional, rather than additive, change in learning. This formal operation turns the empirical question about Early LPP into alternative hypotheses about where one proportional slope should apply.

The No-LPP models fixed both slopes at zero. The General-LPP models estimated (\kappa) and fixed (\kappa_{\mathrm{emot}}=0), giving negative and neutral items the same LPP slope. The Emotion-dependent-LPP models fixed (\kappa=0) and estimated (\kappa_{\mathrm{emot}}), so Early LPP modified source learning only for negative items. General and Emotion-dependent models therefore estimated the same number of parameters; they differed in the items to which their one Early-LPP slope applied. The empirical interaction motivated this contrast, but did not decide it in advance. Crossing the three LPP mappings with the categorical multiplier produced the primary six-model comparison.

<!-- The planned conceptual mechanism figure can be inserted here. Its prose
should preserve the two distinct routes: a categorical multiplier and the chain
Early LPP -> source context-to-item learning strength -> conditional recalled-set
membership. -->

### Primary model comparison

The model grid in @tbl-primary-ecmr-comparison provides the map for the analyses that follow. The rows ask whether a fitted categorical multiplier contributes beyond the source-category representation shared by all models. The columns ask whether Early LPP is omitted, applied generally, or applied only to negative items. Reading the grid as two experimental factors, rather than as six unrelated model names, makes each planned comparison explicit.

{{< include work/lpp_model_comparison/model_comparison_overview.qmd >}}

Three families of comparisons answer the three modeling questions. Within each column, comparing the two rows tests the contribution of the categorical multiplier while holding the LPP mapping fixed. Within each row, comparing either LPP model with its No-LPP parent tests whether Early LPP improves prediction while holding categorical enhancement fixed. Finally, comparing General with Emotion-dependent LPP tests two equal-complexity mappings of the neural measure. We did not include a model that estimated both LPP slopes simultaneously, so this last contrast asks where one slope is most useful; it does not test whether a residual general component is exactly zero after an emotion-dependent component is fitted. All six models retain the same source-context architecture, which isolates these focal terms but does not establish that Early LPP acts specifically through source rather than temporal context. Having defined what the comparisons can decide, we next describe the common evidence used to decide them.

### Pooled estimation and evaluation

Each model was fitted once to the complete set of 342 study-test lists from 38 participants, comprising 6,840 modeled items. One shared parameter vector was estimated per model, using the preprocessed and imputed event-level Early-LPP values described above. Pooling asks whether one set of parameters captures the population-level recalled sets; it does not estimate participant-specific mechanisms or individual differences. Because the available recall vectors identify which items were recalled but do not preserve usable output order, the fitting objective treated each list's responses as an unordered recalled set.

For each list, the model was given the observed number of recalled items and evaluated on which studied items composed that set. We approximated the probability of the unordered set by evaluating 50 fixed random orders of its recalled items and averaging their sequential probabilities. The same sampled orders and random seed were used for every model, making the approximate objective deterministic and paired across comparisons. Termination events were excluded from the likelihood: the model therefore predicts the identities of items entering the recalled set conditional on the observed number recalled. It does not explain why a participant recalled that many items, and it does not test a process for recall stopping.

We minimized the pooled negative log-likelihood with differential evolution [@storn1997differential]. Each model used Latin-hypercube initialization, a maximum of 1,000 generations, and three optimization starts; the lowest negative log-likelihood found across the starts was retained. The Early-LPP slopes were constrained to be nonnegative, reflecting the directional hypothesis from the empirical results, and bounded between 0 and 0.2145. At the largest observed centered Early LPP, this upper bound limits either fitted LPP term to a tenfold learning multiplier. We compared the best-found solutions with Akaike's information criterion, (\mathrm{AIC}=2\mathrm{NLL}+2p), where (p) is the number of free parameters [@burnham2002model]. AIC therefore requires an additional parameter to improve the conditional likelihood enough to justify its flexibility. Because all AIC values derive from the same fixed-order approximation, we interpret their differences rather than their absolute scale.

Likelihood and AIC determine which model best predicts recalled-item identity, but they do not by themselves show which empirical regularity a mechanism reproduces. We therefore generated 200 simulated repetitions of every observed list from each pooled parameter vector, stopping each simulated list after its observed number of recalls. We then summarized the simulated negative-versus-neutral recall difference and the within-list centered Early-LPP difference between Remembered and Forgotten items. These summaries were not additional terms in the fitting objective. They reorganize the same observations that informed model construction and estimation, so they are explanatory diagnostics rather than independent validation. The model-comparison results first establish which mechanisms improved prediction; the diagnostics then clarify what each mechanism contributed.

## Model-comparison results

### Overall comparison

The model combining categorical enhancement with Emotion-dependent Early-LPP modulation had the lowest AIC (@tbl-model-fit). Its negative log-likelihood was 8240.310 with 13 free parameters, yielding an AIC of 16506.619. The complete table is important because the preferred model could arise from one dominant term or from consistent contributions across the grid. The planned contrasts show that both focal mechanisms contributed.

| Model | Categorical multiplier | LPP mapping | Free parameters | NLL | AIC | $\Delta$AIC |
|---|---:|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Source-context baseline | Fixed at 1 | None | 11 | 8313.531 | 16649.063 | 142.443 |
| General-LPP model | Fixed at 1 | General | 12 | 8300.740 | 16625.480 | 118.861 |
| Emotion-dependent-LPP model | Fixed at 1 | Emotion-dependent | 12 | 8280.693 | 16585.387 | 78.768 |
| Categorical-enhancement model | Estimated | None | 12 | 8255.712 | 16535.424 | 28.805 |
| Categorical + General-LPP model | Estimated | General | 13 | 8251.121 | 16528.242 | 21.623 |
| **Categorical + Emotion-dependent-LPP model** | **Estimated** | **Emotion-dependent** | **13** | **8240.310** | **16506.619** | **0.000** |

: Pooled fit of the six primary reduced-eCMR models. {#tbl-model-fit}

*Note.* NLL is the best-found negative log-likelihood across three differential-evolution starts. $\Delta$AIC is relative to the lowest-AIC model. Absolute NLL and AIC values use the common 50-order approximation described in the text; pairwise differences are unaffected by the model-independent scaling constant omitted by averaging order probabilities.

### Planned contrasts

Categorical enhancement improved prediction in every applicable comparison. Relative to the corresponding model with (\phi_{\mathrm{emot}}=1), estimating the categorical multiplier lowered AIC by 113.639 points with no LPP, by 97.238 points with General LPP, and by 78.768 points with Emotion-dependent LPP. The categorical contribution was therefore not made redundant by either use of the neural measure. This consistent row-wise result answers the first question: preferential categorical learning contributed information that item-level Early LPP alone did not supply.

Early LPP also improved prediction in both categorical conditions. Without an estimated categorical multiplier, General LPP lowered AIC by 23.582 points and Emotion-dependent LPP lowered it by 63.676 points relative to the source-context baseline. With the categorical multiplier estimated, the corresponding improvements were 7.182 and 28.805 points. The Early-LPP contribution therefore remained detectable after the model had already captured a category-wide difference in learning. The remaining question was whether the one LPP slope should be shared by negative and neutral items or concentrated among negative items.

Emotion-dependent LPP was preferred to General LPP at equal complexity in both rows of the grid. Concentrating the slope among negative items lowered AIC by 40.094 points when the categorical multiplier was fixed and by 21.623 points when it was estimated. Within the declared coefficient bounds, the set-likelihood comparison therefore favored an emotion-dependent mapping over a common mapping. This is not evidence that a residual general component is zero, because a two-slope model was outside the primary comparison. It is evidence that, when the alternatives were given one LPP coefficient each, the coefficient was more useful when applied to negative items.

The fitted focal parameters are shown in @tbl-focal-parameters. The source-learning scale (\omega) remained free in every model, while fixed entries identify the neutral values that define each comparison. The estimates also show that the preferred Categorical + Emotion-dependent model combined a categorical multiplier of 1.946 with an emotion-dependent log-learning slope of 0.128.

| Model | $\omega$ | $\phi_{\mathrm{emot}}$ | $\kappa$ | $\kappa_{\mathrm{emot}}$ |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Source-context baseline | 0.226 | 1 (fixed) | 0 (fixed) | 0 (fixed) |
| General-LPP model | 9.047 | 1 (fixed) | 0.060 | 0 (fixed) |
| Emotion-dependent-LPP model | 1.385 | 1 (fixed) | 0 (fixed) | 0.184 |
| Categorical-enhancement model | 1.452 | 1.504 | 0 (fixed) | 0 (fixed) |
| Categorical + General-LPP model | 1.037 | 7.525 | 0.208 | 0 (fixed) |
| Categorical + Emotion-dependent-LPP model | 0.930 | 1.946 | 0 (fixed) | 0.128 |

: Fitted focal source-learning parameters. {#tbl-focal-parameters}

One estimate limits the scope of the General-versus-Emotion-dependent conclusion. In the Categorical + General model, (\kappa=0.208), or 97% of its upper bound of 0.2145. We did not fit a wider-bound sensitivity model. The lower AIC of the equal-complexity Emotion-dependent model is therefore a result within the prespecified parameter space, not a bound-independent rejection of a common Early-LPP relationship. The simulation diagnostics provide a complementary, more interpretable view of what the fitted categorical and LPP terms did inside that space.

### Diagnostic predictions

The diagnostics separate the two empirical patterns that motivated the model (@tbl-model-diagnostics). The first column is the negative-minus-neutral recall-rate difference. The other columns report, separately by category, mean within-list centered Early LPP for Remembered items minus that for Forgotten items. Simulations used the observed Early-LPP values attached to each studied item; model parameters changed which items were recalled, and therefore which observed values entered the Remembered and Forgotten groups.

| Model or data | Negative-neutral recall difference | Negative LPP: Remembered - Forgotten | Neutral LPP: Remembered - Forgotten |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Observed data | 0.124 | 0.403 | 0.021 |
| Source-context baseline | 0.001 | 0.003 | 0.004 |
| General-LPP model | 0.007 | 0.211 | 0.184 |
| Emotion-dependent-LPP model | 0.019 | 0.447 | 0.013 |
| Categorical-enhancement model | 0.118 | 0.006 | -0.001 |
| Categorical + General-LPP model | 0.134 | 0.317 | 0.059 |
| Categorical + Emotion-dependent-LPP model | 0.130 | 0.336 | 0.007 |

: Observed and simulated diagnostic point summaries. {#tbl-model-diagnostics}

*Note.* Each simulated value pools 200 repetitions of the 342 observed list structures from the corresponding pooled fit. LPP differences use the same within-list centered Early-LPP predictor as the fitted models. Values are descriptive point summaries, not independent tests or uncertainty intervals.

The category-recall diagnostic identifies the contribution of categorical enhancement. The observed negative-versus-neutral recall difference was 0.124. Models with the categorical multiplier produced differences between 0.118 and 0.134, whereas models without it produced differences between 0.001 and 0.019. Early LPP alone therefore did not reproduce the overall category advantage in this source-context comparison. The fitted categorical multiplier primarily supplied category-level recall structure, which leads to the distinct question of what the Early-LPP term added within categories.

The subsequent-memory diagnostic identifies that item-level contribution. In the observed data, the Remembered-minus-Forgotten centered Early-LPP difference was 0.403 for negative items and 0.021 for neutral items. The No-LPP models produced little separation in either category. General-LPP models produced separation for both negative and neutral items, whereas Emotion-dependent-LPP models concentrated the separation among negative items. The Categorical + Emotion-dependent model produced a negative-item difference of 0.336, a neutral-item difference of 0.007, and retained a category-recall difference of 0.130. The two fitted mechanisms therefore reproduced different parts of the empirical pattern.

### What the comparison establishes

Taken together, the fitted models link measured Early LPP to source context-to-item learning strength and, through that learning strength, to an item's conditional probability of entering the recalled set. Categorical enhancement and Early-LPP modulation contributed complementary structure: categorical enhancement captured the higher recall rate for negative than neutral items, whereas Emotion-dependent Early-LPP modulation captured which negative items were preferentially recalled. This synthesis preserves the mechanistic advance over the preceding empirical association without treating the neural measure as a direct measurement of (\phi_{\mathrm{emot}}) or as a substitute for category information.

The inference is deliberately narrower than a general account of emotional memory. It concerns pooled recalled-item selection within 20-item lists, using within-list centered Early LPP and one reduced source-context implementation. The comparison does not predict total recall or stopping, estimate between-list mean-LPP effects, characterize participant-specific parameters, compare temporal with source pathways, or identify Early LPP uniquely with attention, arousal, or stimulus significance. Those questions require different data or model comparisons. The Discussion can therefore begin from a bounded conclusion: a category-wide learning multiplier and an emotion-dependent item-level neural multiplier jointly accounted for complementary structure in the observed recalled sets.
